title: The U.S. Is Losing the AI Credibility War—to Itself | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Erratic US AI export restrictions—most visibly the worldwide disabling of Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 after a Trump administration export ban—are undermining allied confidence in the US AI stack, giving China an opening as Canadian PM Carney warned G7 that the export ban demonstrates the danger of relying on US models, while closing the 'cyber window of opportunity' to harden Western infrastructure with AI-powered defense tools.

insights.claim: The Trump administration barred foreign nationals from accessing Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models; unable to screen users by nationality, Anthropic disabled both models worldwide—causing widespread uncertainty in the cybersecurity market and squandering a window to harden US and allied networks.
insights.speaker: Matthew Ferren
insights.so_what: Unpredictable US AI export controls undermine the commercial and strategic value of the US AI stack—companies and governments considering US AI infrastructure must now price in the risk of sudden global shutdowns.
insights.locator: President Donald Trump's administration barred foreign nationals

insights.claim: Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney reportedly warned G7 leaders that the Anthropic export ban underscores the dangers of relying on US AI models—a signal that US AI restrictions are actively pushing allies toward considering Chinese or domestic alternatives.
insights.speaker: Matthew Ferren
insights.so_what: US AI companies face an allies-defection risk if export control policy remains unpredictable; the commercial case for US AI adoption by allied governments is inseparable from the reliability and consistency of US policy.
insights.quote: Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney reportedly warned Group of Seven (G7) leaders that the export ban underscores the dangers of relying on U.S. models.
insights.locator: This erratic approach to AI risk mitigation

insights.claim: Ferren argues allies will not adopt a US AI model if its performance can be silently throttled by its developer or if the US government restricts access without warning or coordination—threatening the Pax Silica initiative (State Department's flagship US-led global AI ecosystem effort).
insights.speaker: Matthew Ferren
insights.so_what: The Pax Silica initiative requires a transparent and consistent AI access policy as its foundational precondition; without it, the US forfeits leadership of the global AI ecosystem to whichever provider offers more reliable access.
insights.quote: Allies will not adopt a model if its performance can be silently throttled by its developer
insights.locator: If U.S. policymakers want foreign partners to adopt the U.S. AI stack

insights.claim: The author argues AI tools promise to shift cyber dynamics in favor of defenders—but realizing this requires putting capabilities in the hands of as many defenders as possible, even if malicious actors gain access too, consistent with the longstanding information security principle that transparency and shared resilience outweigh the risks of broader access.
insights.speaker: Matthew Ferren
insights.so_what: Overly restrictive AI export controls paradoxically harm US cyber defense more than they help—the marginal risk of adversary access to AI tools is lower than the cost of leaving defenders under-equipped.
insights.quote: Realizing this outcome requires putting these capabilities in the hands of as many defenders as possible, even if malicious actors also gain access along the way.
insights.locator: AI tools promise to shift the dynamics

insights.claim: China's leading AI models are estimated to be approximately three to eight months behind the most advanced US models; even if the US maintains relative advantage, Chinese offensive cyber capabilities will continue to mature—making the current window for US cyber defense hardening time-limited.
insights.speaker: Matthew Ferren
insights.so_what: The 3-8 month model gap is closing rapidly; the US cybersecurity window to exploit AI defensive advantage is measured in months, not years—policy delays compound the opportunity cost.
insights.quote: Estimates put China's leading models at roughly three to eight months behind the most advanced U.S. models.
